subject: Evidence
coverage_standard: NCBE-aligned Evidence rule bank keyed to the Federal Rules of Evidence as currently in effect. The official NCBE MBE Subject Matter Outline states that all Evidence questions should be answered according to the Federal Rules of Evidence and allocates approximately one-quarter of Evidence questions to Presentation of Evidence, one-third to Relevancy and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence, one-quarter to Hearsay, and the remainder to Witnesses and Contents of Writings/Recordings/Photographs.

official_scope_reference.source_note: All Evidence questions should be answered according to the Federal Rules of Evidence, as currently in effect.
official_scope_reference.distribution_note: Approximately one-quarter of Evidence questions are based on category I, one-third on category II, one-quarter on category V, and the remainder on categories III and IV.

rules.title: Judicial Notice
rules.prompt_question: When may a court take judicial notice of a fact?
rules.rule_statement: A court may judicially notice a fact that is not subject to reasonable dispute because it is either generally known within the trial court's territorial jurisdiction or can be accurately and readily determined from sources whose accuracy cannot reasonably be questioned.
rules.application_example: A court may judicially notice that July 4 is a federal holiday.
rules.common_trap: In a civil case, the jury must accept a judicially noticed fact as conclusive, but in a criminal case the jury may accept or reject it.

rules.title: Personal Knowledge
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a witness to testify about a matter?
rules.rule_statement: A witness may testify only if evidence is introduced sufficient to support a finding that the witness has personal knowledge of the matter.
rules.application_example: An eyewitness may testify about what she saw at the intersection.
rules.common_trap: The witness need not have absolute certainty; enough evidence must exist for a reasonable juror to find personal knowledge.

rules.title: Refreshing Recollection
rules.prompt_question: How may a witness's recollection be refreshed?
rules.rule_statement: A witness's recollection may be refreshed with almost any item, whether or not admissible, so long as the witness then testifies from present recollection. The adverse party is entitled to inspect the item, cross-examine the witness about it, and introduce relevant portions into evidence.
rules.application_example: A witness reviews her notes silently, remembers, and then testifies from memory.
rules.common_trap: The writing used to refresh is not itself admitted merely because it was used; if the witness still cannot remember, recorded recollection may apply instead.

rules.title: Recorded Recollection
rules.prompt_question: When is a recorded recollection admissible?
rules.rule_statement: A record may be read into evidence as a recorded recollection if the witness once knew about the matter but now cannot recall well enough to testify fully and accurately, the record was made or adopted when the matter was fresh in memory, and the record accurately reflects the witness's knowledge. The exhibit itself is received only if offered by the adverse party.
rules.application_example: A police officer cannot remember details at trial, but her contemporaneous report may be read into evidence.
rules.common_trap: Recorded recollection is read into evidence, not usually received as an exhibit unless the opponent offers it.

rules.title: Objections and Offers of Proof
rules.prompt_question: How are evidentiary errors preserved for appeal?
rules.rule_statement: To preserve error in admitting evidence, a party must timely object or move to strike and state the specific ground unless apparent from the context. To preserve error in excluding evidence, a party must inform the court of the substance of the evidence by an offer of proof unless the substance is apparent from the context.
rules.application_example: Counsel objects to hearsay as soon as the question is asked.
rules.common_trap: A general objection may be insufficient unless the ground is obvious.

rules.title: Plain Error
rules.prompt_question: When may an appellate court review an unpreserved evidentiary error?
rules.rule_statement: A court may take notice of a plain error affecting a substantial right even if the claim of error was not properly preserved.
rules.application_example: A highly prejudicial constitutional error may be reviewed despite no objection.
rules.common_trap: Plain-error review is discretionary and applied sparingly.

rules.title: Lay Opinion
rules.prompt_question: When is lay opinion testimony admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Lay opinion testimony is admissible if it is rationally based on the witness's perception, helpful to understanding the witness's testimony or determining a fact in issue, and not based on scientific, technical, or other specialized knowledge within Rule 702.
rules.application_example: A lay witness may testify that a person appeared drunk or upset.
rules.common_trap: A lay witness cannot offer expert-style technical conclusions.

rules.title: Preliminary Questions of Admissibility
rules.prompt_question: Who decides preliminary questions about admissibility?
rules.rule_statement: The judge decides preliminary questions about whether a witness is qualified, a privilege exists, or evidence is admissible, and in doing so is not bound by evidence rules except those on privilege.
keywords: not bound by rules except privilege
rules.application_example: The court decides whether a hearsay exception has been established.
rules.common_trap: Conditional relevance is different: the judge asks only whether sufficient proof exists for a reasonable juror to find the preliminary fact.

rules.title: Rule 403 Balancing
rules.prompt_question: When may relevant evidence be excluded under Rule 403?
rules.rule_statement: Relevant evidence may be excluded if its probative value is substantially outweighed by a danger of unfair prejudice, confusing the issues, misleading the jury, undue delay, wasting time, or needlessly presenting cumulative evidence.
rules.application_example: Graphic photos may be excluded if their emotional impact substantially outweighs their probative value.
rules.common_trap: The standard is substantially outweighed, so exclusion under Rule 403 is meant to be used sparingly.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Basic Relevance
rules.prompt_question: What makes evidence relevant?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence is relevant if it has any tendency to make a fact more or less probable than it would be without the evidence and the fact is of consequence in determining the action.
rules.application_example: Evidence that the defendant was near the scene tends to make identity more probable.
rules.common_trap: Relevance is a very low threshold, but other rules may still exclude relevant evidence.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Conditional Relevance
rules.prompt_question: What is conditional relevance?
rules.rule_statement: When the relevance of evidence depends on whether a fact exists, the evidence is admissible if sufficient proof has been introduced to support a finding that the fact exists.
keywords: depends on whether a fact exists
rules.application_example: A gun is relevant only if the jury could reasonably find it was the gun used in the crime.
rules.common_trap: The judge does not decide the fact conclusively; the judge only screens for sufficient evidence.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Subsequent Remedial Measures
rules.prompt_question: When are subsequent remedial measures inadmissible?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of measures that would have made an earlier injury or harm less likely to occur is not admissible to prove negligence, culpable conduct, a defect in a product or its design, or a need for a warning or instruction. But it may be admissible for another purpose, such as impeachment or proving ownership, control, or the feasibility of precautionary measures if disputed.
rules.application_example: After an accident, the store installs handrails; that evidence is inadmissible to prove negligence.
rules.common_trap: The rule applies only to measures taken after the event and only to specified forbidden inferences.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Compromise Offers and Negotiations
rules.prompt_question: When are settlement offers and negotiations inadmissible?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of furnishing, promising, or offering valuable consideration to compromise a disputed claim, and conduct or statements made during compromise negotiations, is not admissible to prove or disprove the validity or amount of the claim or to impeach by prior inconsistent statement or contradiction. It may be admissible for another purpose, such as proving bias, prejudice, or obstruction.
rules.application_example: An offer to pay medical bills in exchange for settlement cannot be used to prove liability.
rules.common_trap: The underlying facts are discoverable and admissible; only the settlement communications are restricted.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Offers to Pay Medical Expenses
rules.prompt_question: Are offers to pay medical expenses admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of furnishing, promising to pay, or offering to pay medical, hospital, or similar expenses resulting from an injury is not admissible to prove liability for the injury.
rules.application_example: A defendant's statement 'I'll pay your hospital bills' cannot be used as an admission of liability.
rules.common_trap: Unlike settlement negotiations, only the offer itself is excluded; accompanying statements of fault may still be admissible unless another rule excludes them.

rules.topic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Pleas, Plea Discussions, and Related Statements
rules.prompt_question: When are plea discussions inadmissible?
rules.rule_statement: In a civil or criminal case, evidence of a withdrawn guilty plea, a nolo contendere plea, statements made during a proceeding on either plea, or statements made during plea discussions with the prosecuting authority that did not result in a guilty plea or resulted in a later-withdrawn guilty plea is generally inadmissible against the defendant.
rules.application_example: A defendant's abandoned plea bargain statements cannot be used against him at trial.
rules.common_trap: The protection generally applies only to discussions with prosecutors or their agents, not casual talks with police absent prosecutorial authority.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Liability Insurance
rules.prompt_question: When is evidence of liability insurance inadmissible?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence that a person was or was not insured against liability is not admissible to prove whether the person acted negligently or otherwise wrongfully, but it may be admissible for another purpose, such as proving bias, agency, ownership, or control.
rules.application_example: Evidence that the defendant carried insurance cannot be used to show the defendant likely caused the accident.
rules.common_trap: The rule does not bar insurance evidence offered for a permissible noncharacter purpose.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Character Evidence and Propensity
rules.prompt_question: What is the general rule on using character evidence to show conduct?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of a person's character or character trait is generally not admissible to prove that on a particular occasion the person acted in accordance with the character or trait.
rules.application_example: Evidence that a driver is generally careless is not admissible to prove he drove carelessly on the day in question.
rules.common_trap: Character evidence is often admissible for other purposes, such as impeachment or when character is directly in issue.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Methods of Proving Character
rules.prompt_question: How may character be proved when admissible?
rules.rule_statement: When evidence of a person's character or character trait is admissible, it may be proved by testimony about reputation or by testimony in the form of an opinion. On cross-examination of the character witness, the court may allow inquiry into relevant specific instances of conduct. When character is an essential element of a charge, claim, or defense, specific instances are also admissible.
rules.application_example: In a defamation case where truth about character is directly in issue, specific acts may be used.
rules.common_trap: Specific acts are generally not permitted to prove conduct in conformity unless character itself is an essential element.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Character Evidence in Criminal Cases
rules.prompt_question: When may a criminal defendant offer character evidence?
rules.rule_statement: A criminal defendant may offer evidence of the defendant's pertinent trait, and if admitted, the prosecutor may rebut it. A defendant may also offer evidence of an alleged victim's pertinent trait, subject to important limits.
rules.application_example: A defendant charged with assault may offer evidence of peacefulness.
rules.common_trap: The prosecution generally cannot initiate propensity character evidence against the defendant unless the defendant opens the door.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Homicide Case Peacefulness Exception
rules.prompt_question: When may the prosecutor offer evidence of a homicide victim's peacefulness?
rules.rule_statement: In a homicide case, the prosecutor may offer evidence of the alleged victim's trait of peacefulness to rebut evidence that the victim was the first aggressor.
rules.application_example: Defendant claims the victim attacked first; prosecution responds with evidence the victim was peaceful.
rules.common_trap: This rebuttal is available even if the defendant did not first attack the victim's character by reputation or opinion evidence.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Rape Shield Rule
rules.prompt_question: What does the rape shield rule prohibit?
rules.rule_statement: In civil and criminal proceedings involving alleged sexual misconduct, evidence offered to prove that a victim engaged in other sexual behavior or evidence offered to prove a victim's sexual predisposition is generally inadmissible, subject to limited exceptions.
rules.application_example: A defendant in a sexual-assault case generally cannot introduce the victim's prior sexual history to suggest consent.
rules.common_trap: In criminal cases, exceptions include specific instances to prove another source of semen or injury and, in some cases, prior sexual behavior with the accused to prove consent.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Other Crimes, Wrongs, or Acts
rules.prompt_question: When may other acts evidence be admitted?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of a crime, wrong, or other act is not admissible to prove a person's character in order to show that on a particular occasion the person acted in accordance with the character, but it may be admissible for another purpose, such as proving motive, opportunity, intent, preparation, plan, knowledge, identity, absence of mistake, or lack of accident.
rules.application_example: Prior nearly identical burglaries may be admissible to prove identity through a distinctive modus operandi.
rules.common_trap: The court must still apply Rule 403 balancing, and in criminal cases reasonable pretrial notice may be required.

rules.subtopic: Relevance and Reasons for Excluding Relevant Evidence
rules.title: Habit and Routine Practice
rules.prompt_question: When is habit evidence admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of a person's habit or an organization's routine practice may be admitted to prove that on a particular occasion the person or organization acted in accordance with the habit or routine practice.
rules.application_example: Evidence that a driver always fastens his seatbelt before driving may be admissible as habit.
rules.common_trap: Habit is more specific and regular than general character.

rules.title: General Competency
rules.prompt_question: Who is competent to be a witness?
rules.rule_statement: Every person is competent to be a witness unless the rules provide otherwise. Competency usually requires personal knowledge, an oath or affirmation, and the ability to perceive, remember, and communicate.
rules.application_example: A child may testify if capable of understanding and relating events truthfully.
rules.common_trap: Questions about witness capacity usually go to weight unless the witness truly cannot meet minimum competency standards.

rules.title: Judge and Juror as Witnesses
rules.prompt_question: May the trial judge or jurors testify in the trial?
rules.rule_statement: The presiding judge may not testify as a witness at the trial, and a juror may not testify as a witness before the other jurors at the trial. A party need not object to preserve the issue as to the judge.
rules.application_example: The trial judge cannot step down and offer factual testimony in the same case.
rules.common_trap: Separate post-verdict rules govern juror testimony about verdict validity.

rules.title: Dead Man's Statute
rules.prompt_question: What is a Dead Man's Statute?
rules.rule_statement: Under some state dead man's statutes, an interested survivor may be barred from testifying about communications or transactions with a deceased person when offered against the deceased's estate. Federal courts apply state competency law in civil cases where state law supplies the rule of decision.
rules.application_example: In diversity, a state's dead man's statute may limit a survivor's testimony against the decedent's estate.
rules.common_trap: There is no federal dead man's statute.

rules.title: Expert Qualification
rules.prompt_question: When may a witness testify as an expert?
rules.rule_statement: A witness who is qualified as an expert by knowledge, skill, experience, training, or education may testify in the form of an opinion if the expert's specialized knowledge will help the trier of fact, the testimony is based on sufficient facts or data, the testimony is the product of reliable principles and methods, and the expert has reliably applied the principles and methods to the facts of the case.
rules.application_example: A forensic accountant may explain complex tracing analysis to the jury.
rules.common_trap: The judge serves as gatekeeper on reliability under Rule 702.

rules.title: Basis of Expert Opinion
rules.prompt_question: What may an expert base an opinion on?
rules.rule_statement: An expert may base an opinion on facts or data in the case that the expert has been made aware of or personally observed, and if experts in the field would reasonably rely on such facts or data, the information need not itself be admissible. But inadmissible facts or data may be disclosed to the jury only if their probative value in helping evaluate the opinion substantially outweighs their prejudicial effect.
rules.application_example: A physician may rely on medical records prepared by others.
rules.common_trap: The expert cannot become a mere conduit for otherwise inadmissible hearsay.

rules.title: Ultimate Issue
rules.prompt_question: May opinion testimony embrace an ultimate issue?
rules.rule_statement: Opinion testimony is not objectionable just because it embraces an ultimate issue, but in a criminal case an expert may not state an opinion about whether the defendant did or did not have a mental state or condition that constitutes an element of the crime or defense.
rules.application_example: An accident expert may opine that a collision was caused by excessive speed.
rules.common_trap: Experts cannot tell the jury directly that the defendant had the required mens rea.

rules.title: Opinion on Credibility
rules.prompt_question: May a witness opine that another witness is telling the truth?
rules.rule_statement: A witness generally may not directly opine that another witness is truthful on a particular occasion, because credibility determinations are for the jury.
rules.application_example: An expert cannot testify that a child-victim is telling the truth about abuse.
rules.common_trap: General character-for-truthfulness evidence is governed by impeachment and rehabilitation rules, not direct opinions on a specific story.

rules.title: Leading Questions
rules.prompt_question: When are leading questions permitted?
rules.rule_statement: Leading questions should not be used on direct examination except as necessary to develop the witness's testimony, but they are ordinarily allowed on cross-examination and with hostile witnesses, adverse parties, or witnesses identified with an adverse party.
rules.application_example: Counsel may lead an adverse witness called during plaintiff's case-in-chief.
rules.common_trap: The trial judge has discretion to allow leading questions for preliminary matters or a witness with communication difficulties.

rules.title: Scope of Cross-Examination
rules.prompt_question: What is the scope of cross-examination?
rules.rule_statement: Cross-examination should not go beyond the subject matter of the direct examination and matters affecting the witness's credibility, although the court may allow inquiry into additional matters as if on direct examination.
rules.application_example: A defense lawyer may cross the eyewitness on visibility and bias.
rules.common_trap: If the examiner ventures into new matters, the court may treat that part as direct examination for purposes such as leading questions.

rules.title: Use of Writings on Cross
rules.prompt_question: How may a cross-examiner use a writing with a witness?
rules.rule_statement: When examining a witness about a prior statement or other writing, the examiner need not show it or disclose its contents to the witness at that time, but on request must show it or disclose its contents to opposing counsel.
keywords: must show opposing counsel on request
rules.application_example: Counsel can surprise a witness with prior inconsistent deposition testimony.
rules.common_trap: This procedural rule is separate from the substantive requirements for admitting the prior statement.

rules.title: Hostile Witness
rules.prompt_question: How may a party treat a hostile witness?
rules.rule_statement: The court may permit a party to use leading questions with a hostile witness, an adverse party, or a witness identified with an adverse party.
rules.application_example: A plaintiff may lead a defendant's employee who is aligned with the defense.
rules.common_trap: The trial judge has discretion in determining hostility.

rules.title: Privilege Law in Federal Court
rules.prompt_question: What law governs privileges in federal court?
rules.rule_statement: In a federal question case, privileges are governed by federal common law unless the Constitution, a federal statute, or rules prescribed by the Supreme Court provide otherwise. In a civil case where state law supplies the rule of decision, state privilege law applies.
keywords: state law supplies the rule of decision
rules.application_example: In diversity, state physician-patient privilege rules may apply.
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse privilege rules with hearsay or relevance rules, which are usually governed by the FRE even in diversity.

rules.title: Attorney-Client Privilege
rules.prompt_question: What does the attorney-client privilege protect?
rules.rule_statement: The attorney-client privilege protects confidential communications between client and attorney, or their representatives, made for the purpose of obtaining or providing legal advice.
rules.application_example: A client privately emails her lawyer seeking advice about a contract dispute.
rules.common_trap: The privilege protects communications, not underlying facts, and the presence of unnecessary third parties usually destroys confidentiality.

rules.title: Waiver of Attorney-Client Privilege
rules.prompt_question: How may the attorney-client privilege be waived?
rules.rule_statement: The attorney-client privilege may be waived by voluntary disclosure of the privileged communication to a third party, by failure to maintain confidentiality, or by placing the communication at issue.
rules.application_example: Forwarding privileged advice to a business partner may waive the privilege.
rules.common_trap: Disclosure reasonably necessary to facilitate the legal advice, such as use of an interpreter, usually does not waive the privilege.

rules.title: Crime-Fraud Exception
rules.prompt_question: When does the crime-fraud exception apply?
rules.rule_statement: The attorney-client privilege does not apply to communications made for the purpose of seeking assistance to commit or cover up a future crime or fraud.
rules.application_example: A client asks a lawyer how to disguise embezzled funds.
rules.common_trap: The exception does not apply merely because the client has committed a past crime and seeks advice about legal consequences.

rules.title: Physician and Psychotherapist Privileges
rules.prompt_question: What privileges may protect medical communications?
rules.rule_statement: Under federal common law, there is no general physician-patient privilege, but there is a psychotherapist-patient privilege protecting confidential communications for diagnosis or treatment of mental or emotional conditions. In diversity cases, state privilege law may recognize broader privileges.
rules.application_example: Communications with a licensed therapist are privileged in federal question cases.
rules.common_trap: Ordinary medical treatment communications are not privileged under federal common law unless state law controls.

rules.title: Spousal Testimonial Privilege
rules.prompt_question: What is the spousal testimonial privilege?
rules.rule_statement: In criminal cases, the witness-spouse may refuse to testify adversely against the defendant-spouse during the marriage. The privilege belongs to the witness-spouse and ends upon divorce.
rules.application_example: A wife called by the government may refuse to testify against her husband while they are still married.
rules.common_trap: The privilege does not apply in civil cases and covers testimony, not merely confidential communications.

rules.title: Marital Communications Privilege
rules.prompt_question: What does the marital communications privilege protect?
rules.rule_statement: The marital communications privilege protects confidential communications made between spouses during a valid marriage. Either spouse may assert the privilege, and it survives divorce as to communications made during the marriage.
rules.application_example: A husband's private confession to his wife during marriage is privileged.
rules.common_trap: The privilege covers only communications, not observations, and there are exceptions such as certain disputes between spouses or crimes against the spouse or children.

rules.title: Inadvertent Disclosure
rules.prompt_question: When does inadvertent disclosure waive privilege?
rules.rule_statement: In federal proceedings, inadvertent disclosure does not operate as a waiver if the disclosure was inadvertent, the holder of the privilege or protection took reasonable steps to prevent disclosure, and promptly took reasonable steps to rectify the error.
rules.application_example: A privileged memo accidentally produced in discovery may remain protected if promptly clawed back.
rules.common_trap: State rules may differ unless federal law governs and the court applies FRE 502 or related federal standards.

rules.title: Subsequent Similar Accidents
rules.prompt_question: When are subsequent similar accidents admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of subsequent accidents may be admissible to prove a dangerous condition, causation, or notice if sufficiently similar and not barred by some specific exclusion; unlike subsequent remedial measures, subsequent accidents are not categorically excluded.
rules.application_example: Evidence that others slipped on the same unmarked stair later that week may help show the stair was dangerous.
rules.common_trap: Similarity and Rule 403 are the main limits here.

rules.topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
rules.title: Best Evidence Rule
rules.prompt_question: When does the best evidence rule apply?
rules.rule_statement: To prove the content of a writing, recording, or photograph, the original is required except as otherwise provided by the rules or statute.
rules.application_example: To prove what a contract said, the original contract is ordinarily required.
rules.common_trap: The rule applies only when a party seeks to prove contents, not when a witness testifies from personal knowledge of an event that also happened to be recorded.

rules.topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
rules.title: Duplicates
rules.prompt_question: When is a duplicate admissible in place of an original?
rules.rule_statement: A duplicate is admissible to the same extent as the original unless there is a genuine question about the original's authenticity or the circumstances make it unfair to admit the duplicate.
rules.application_example: A photocopy of a letter is usually admissible to prove its contents.
rules.common_trap: A duplicate is generally enough; do not demand the physical original absent a specific problem.

rules.topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
rules.title: Lost or Destroyed Originals
rules.prompt_question: When may secondary evidence prove contents?
rules.rule_statement: An original is not required, and other evidence of the content is admissible, if all originals are lost or destroyed and not by the proponent acting in bad faith; an original cannot be obtained by available judicial process; the party against whom it would be offered had control of the original and failed to produce it after notice; or the writing is not closely related to a controlling issue.
rules.application_example: If a receipt was accidentally destroyed in a fire, testimony about its contents may be allowed.
rules.common_trap: Secondary evidence is not limited to the best available secondary source unless a rule or fairness principle requires otherwise.

rules.topic: Contents of Writings, Recordings, and Photographs
rules.title: Public Records and Summaries
rules.prompt_question: How may public records and voluminous documents be proved?
rules.rule_statement: The contents of an official record may be proved by a copy certified as correct or testified to as correct by a witness who has compared it with the original. The contents of voluminous writings, recordings, or photographs that cannot be conveniently examined in court may be presented in the form of a summary, chart, or calculation if the originals or duplicates are made available for examination or copying.
rules.application_example: A summary spreadsheet may be used to present thousands of bank transactions.
rules.common_trap: The underlying documents must be admissible and available to the opposing party.

rules.title: General Authentication Requirement
rules.prompt_question: What is required to authenticate evidence?
rules.rule_statement: To authenticate or identify an item of evidence, the proponent must produce evidence sufficient to support a finding that the item is what the proponent claims it is.
rules.application_example: A witness recognizes a photograph as a fair depiction of the scene.
rules.common_trap: Authentication is a threshold showing; the jury ultimately decides the item's weight.

rules.title: Examples of Authentication
rules.prompt_question: How may evidence be authenticated?
rules.rule_statement: Authentication may be shown by testimony of a witness with knowledge, distinctive characteristics and surrounding circumstances, voice identification, comparison by an expert or the trier of fact, evidence about telephone or electronic communications, chain of custody, and other methods recognized by the rules.
rules.application_example: A text message may be authenticated by its content, sender's number, and reply pattern.
rules.common_trap: Chain of custody need not be perfect if sufficient evidence supports authenticity.

rules.title: Self-Authenticating Evidence
rules.prompt_question: What evidence is self-authenticating?
rules.rule_statement: Certain items require no extrinsic evidence of authenticity, including sealed and signed public documents, certified public records, official publications, newspapers and periodicals, trade inscriptions, acknowledged documents, commercial paper, and certain certified electronic records.
rules.application_example: A notarized deed may be self-authenticating.
rules.common_trap: Self-authentication does not resolve hearsay or relevance objections.

rules.title: Definition of Hearsay
rules.prompt_question: What is hearsay?
rules.rule_statement: Hearsay is an out-of-court statement offered in evidence to prove the truth of the matter asserted in the statement.
rules.application_example: A witness testifies that another person said, 'The light was red,' to prove the light was red.
rules.common_trap: If the statement is offered for a nontruth purpose, it is not hearsay.

rules.title: What Counts as a Statement
rules.prompt_question: What is a statement for hearsay purposes?
rules.rule_statement: A statement is a person's oral assertion, written assertion, or nonverbal conduct if the person intended it as an assertion.
rules.application_example: A nod in response to a question can be a statement if intended as an assertion.
rules.common_trap: Machine-generated outputs and nonassertive conduct are generally not hearsay statements because they are not human assertions.

rules.title: Nonhearsay Uses of Statements
rules.prompt_question: When is an out-of-court statement not hearsay because of the purpose for which it is offered?
rules.rule_statement: A statement is not hearsay if offered for a nontruth purpose, such as to show its effect on the listener, to provide notice, to prove a verbal act or legally operative words, to show the declarant's state of mind circumstantially, or to impeach.
rules.application_example: A warning that the floor was wet is admissible to show the store had notice, not to prove the floor actually was wet.
rules.common_trap: Always ask why the statement is being offered before calling it hearsay.

rules.title: Statement of an Opposing Party
rules.prompt_question: When is an opposing party's statement admissible as nonhearsay?
rules.rule_statement: A statement is defined as not hearsay if it is offered against an opposing party and was made by the party in an individual or representative capacity; is one the party manifested that it adopted or believed true; was made by a person whom the party authorized to make a statement on the subject; was made by the party's agent or employee on a matter within the scope of that relationship and while it existed; or was made by the party's coconspirator during and in furtherance of the conspiracy.
rules.application_example: A company's employee statement about a defect made within the scope of employment may be admissible against the company.
rules.common_trap: These are not hearsay exemptions for the party who made them; they are admissible only when offered against that party.

rules.title: Adoptive Admission
rules.prompt_question: What is an adoptive admission?
rules.rule_statement: A statement is admissible as an adoptive admission if the party manifested an adoption or belief in its truth, including in some circumstances by silence when a reasonable person would deny the statement.
rules.application_example: A defendant nods when a friend says, 'You knew the books were falsified.'
rules.common_trap: Silence as adoption is used cautiously, especially in criminal cases after arrest or after Miranda warnings.

rules.title: Prior Inconsistent Statement
rules.prompt_question: When is a prior inconsistent statement admissible substantively as nonhearsay?
rules.rule_statement: A witness's prior inconsistent statement is defined as not hearsay if the declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination about the statement, and the statement was given under penalty of perjury at a trial, hearing, other proceeding, or deposition.
rules.application_example: A witness's prior inconsistent grand jury testimony may come in for its truth.
rules.common_trap: Unsworn prior inconsistent statements are usually admissible only to impeach, not substantively, unless another hearsay exception applies.

rules.title: Prior Consistent Statement
rules.prompt_question: When is a prior consistent statement admissible substantively as nonhearsay?
rules.rule_statement: A witness's prior consistent statement is defined as not hearsay if the declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination about the statement and the statement is offered to rebut an express or implied charge that the witness recently fabricated it or acted from a recent improper influence or motive, or to rehabilitate credibility when attacked on another ground.
rules.application_example: A witness accused of recently inventing a story may use a prior consistent statement made before the alleged motive to fabricate arose.
rules.common_trap: To rebut fabrication or improper motive, the prior statement generally must predate the alleged motive.

rules.title: Prior Identification
rules.prompt_question: When is a prior identification admissible substantively as nonhearsay?
rules.rule_statement: A declarant-witness's prior statement identifying a person after perceiving that person is defined as not hearsay if the declarant testifies and is subject to cross-examination about the statement.
rules.application_example: A witness's earlier lineup identification is admissible even if the witness cannot identify the defendant in court.
rules.common_trap: The declarant must testify and be subject to cross-examination, though memory loss does not necessarily prevent that.

rules.title: Present Sense Impression
rules.prompt_question: What is the present sense impression exception?
rules.rule_statement: A statement describing or explaining an event or condition, made while or immediately after the declarant perceived it, is admissible as a present sense impression.
rules.application_example: A 911 caller says, 'The blue car is running the red light right now.'
rules.common_trap: The timing must be essentially contemporaneous; later narratives do not qualify.

rules.title: Excited Utterance
rules.prompt_question: What is the excited utterance exception?
rules.rule_statement: A statement relating to a startling event or condition, made while the declarant was under the stress of excitement that it caused, is admissible as an excited utterance.
rules.application_example: Moments after a crash, a victim blurts out, 'That truck ran the stop sign!'
rules.common_trap: The statement must be made while the stress still dominates; the longer the delay, the more doubtful the exception.

rules.title: Then-Existing State of Mind
rules.prompt_question: What is the state-of-mind hearsay exception?
rules.rule_statement: A statement of the declarant's then-existing state of mind, emotion, sensation, or physical condition, such as motive, intent, plan, mental feeling, pain, or bodily health, is admissible. But it does not include a statement of memory or belief to prove the fact remembered or believed unless it relates to the validity or terms of the declarant's will.
rules.application_example: 'I intend to go to Denver tomorrow' may be admissible to show future intent.
rules.common_trap: A statement like 'I believe John poisoned me' is generally inadmissible to prove John poisoned me.

rules.title: Statements for Medical Diagnosis or Treatment
rules.prompt_question: What statements are admissible under the medical diagnosis or treatment exception?
rules.rule_statement: A statement made for, and reasonably pertinent to, medical diagnosis or treatment that describes medical history, past or present symptoms, their inception, or their general cause is admissible.
rules.application_example: A patient tells the emergency physician, 'My shoulder hurts because I was hit from behind in a car accident.'
rules.common_trap: Statements assigning fault are usually not admissible unless relevant to treatment, such as identity in certain child-abuse cases.

rules.title: Recorded Recollection as Hearsay Exception
rules.prompt_question: How does recorded recollection function as a hearsay exception?
rules.rule_statement: A record that meets the recorded recollection requirements is admissible to be read into evidence despite hearsay concerns when the witness cannot remember well enough to testify fully and accurately.
rules.application_example: A witness's contemporaneous written notes may be read to the jury.
rules.common_trap: This exception overlaps with the refreshing-recollection doctrine but applies only when memory cannot be restored.

rules.title: Business Records
rules.prompt_question: When is a business record admissible?
rules.rule_statement: A record of an act, event, condition, opinion, or diagnosis is admissible as a business record if it was made at or near the time by, or from information transmitted by, someone with knowledge; was kept in the course of a regularly conducted business activity; making the record was a regular practice of that activity; these conditions are shown by testimony or certification of a custodian or qualified witness; and the source or circumstances do not indicate a lack of trustworthiness.
rules.application_example: Hospital billing records routinely prepared in the ordinary course may qualify.
rules.common_trap: Police reports offered against a criminal defendant often fail because of the public-records rule or confrontation concerns, and outsider statements in a business record need their own exception.

rules.title: Absence of Business Record
rules.prompt_question: How may the absence of a business record be proved?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence that a matter is not included in a record described by the business-records exception is admissible to prove the matter did not occur or exist if the matter was of a kind regularly recorded and the opponent does not show untrustworthiness.
rules.application_example: Bank records showing no wire transfer entry may be used to prove no transfer occurred.
rules.common_trap: The proponent must show the business regularly recorded such matters.

rules.title: Public Records
rules.prompt_question: When are public records admissible?
rules.rule_statement: A record or statement of a public office is admissible if it sets out the office's activities, a matter observed while under a legal duty to report, or in a civil case or against the government in a criminal case factual findings from a legally authorized investigation, and the opponent does not show lack of trustworthiness.
rules.application_example: A certified birth certificate may qualify as a public record.
rules.common_trap: In criminal cases, matters observed by law-enforcement personnel are generally excluded when offered against the accused, and investigative findings may not be used by the prosecution against the defendant.

rules.title: Absence of Public Record
rules.prompt_question: How may the absence of a public record be proved?
rules.rule_statement: Testimony or certification that a diligent search failed to disclose a public record or statement is admissible to prove that the record or statement does not exist or that a matter did not occur if a public office regularly kept such records.
rules.application_example: A certificate showing no marriage license on file may prove the parties were not legally married.
rules.common_trap: As with absence of business records, there must be a showing that the office would normally record the matter.

rules.title: Family Records
rules.prompt_question: What family records are admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Statements of fact about personal or family history contained in a family record, such as a Bible, genealogy, engraving on a ring, inscription on a portrait, or engraving on an urn or burial marker, are admissible.
rules.application_example: An inscription in a family Bible showing a date of birth may be admitted.
rules.common_trap: This exception is narrow and concerns pedigree-style information.

rules.title: Market Reports and Commercial Publications
rules.prompt_question: When are market reports and commercial publications admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Market quotations, lists, directories, or other compilations generally relied on by the public or by persons in particular occupations are admissible.
rules.application_example: A standard commodity-price listing may be admitted to show market value.
rules.common_trap: The publication must be one generally relied on for accuracy.

rules.title: Ancient Documents Exception
rules.prompt_question: When is a statement in an ancient document admissible?
rules.rule_statement: A statement in a document prepared before January 1, 1998, whose authenticity is established, is admissible under the ancient-documents hearsay exception.
rules.application_example: A century-old deed recital may be admitted under this exception once authenticated.
rules.common_trap: The document must first be authenticated.

rules.title: Learned Treatise
rules.prompt_question: How may a learned treatise be used substantively?
rules.rule_statement: A statement contained in a treatise, periodical, or pamphlet may be read into evidence if called to the attention of an expert witness on cross-examination or relied on by the expert on direct examination, and the publication is established as a reliable authority by the expert, another expert, judicial notice, or other evidence. The statement may be read into evidence but not received as an exhibit.
rules.application_example: A medical textbook passage may be read to challenge an expert's causation opinion.
rules.common_trap: The treatise itself ordinarily does not go to the jury room as an exhibit.

rules.title: Reputation Concerning Personal or Family History
rules.prompt_question: What reputation evidence about family history is admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Reputation among a person's family by blood, adoption, or marriage, or among associates or in the community, concerning a person's birth, adoption, legitimacy, ancestry, marriage, divorce, death, relationship by blood, adoption, or marriage, or similar personal or family history is admissible.
rules.application_example: Community reputation that two people were husband and wife may be admitted on a pedigree issue.
rules.common_trap: This is a hearsay exception even though the source is general reputation, not personal knowledge.

rules.title: Unavailability
rules.prompt_question: When is a declarant unavailable for hearsay purposes?
rules.rule_statement: A declarant is unavailable if the declarant is exempted from testifying due to privilege, refuses despite a court order, testifies to not remembering the subject matter, cannot be present due to death or then-existing physical or mental illness or infirmity, or is absent and the statement's proponent has not been able, by process or other reasonable means, to procure the declarant's attendance or testimony.
rules.application_example: A deceased declarant is unavailable.
rules.common_trap: A party cannot create unavailability by wrongdoing and then benefit from it.

rules.title: Former Testimony
rules.prompt_question: When is former testimony admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Former testimony given as a witness at a trial, hearing, or lawful deposition is admissible if the declarant is unavailable and the party against whom it is now offered, or in a civil case the party's predecessor in interest, had an opportunity and similar motive to develop the testimony by direct, cross-, or redirect examination.
rules.application_example: Deposition testimony of a witness who later dies may be read at trial.
rules.common_trap: In criminal cases, there must be the same party and a prior opportunity and similar motive to examine.

rules.title: Dying Declaration
rules.prompt_question: What is required for a dying declaration?
rules.rule_statement: In a prosecution for homicide or in a civil case, a statement made by a declarant while believing the declarant's death to be imminent, about its cause or circumstances, is admissible if the declarant is unavailable.
rules.application_example: A mortally wounded victim says, 'Dan shot me.'
rules.common_trap: The exception does not apply in nonhomicide criminal prosecutions.

rules.title: Statement Against Interest
rules.prompt_question: When is a statement against interest admissible?
rules.rule_statement: A statement that a reasonable person in the declarant's position would have made only if the person believed it to be true because, when made, it was so contrary to the declarant's proprietary or pecuniary interest or had so great a tendency to invalidate the declarant's claim or to expose the declarant to civil or criminal liability is admissible if the declarant is unavailable. In a criminal case, a statement tending to expose the declarant to criminal liability and offered to exculpate the accused requires corroborating circumstances that clearly indicate trustworthiness.
rules.application_example: A now-deceased bookkeeper admitted, 'I stole the funds myself.'
rules.common_trap: Do not confuse a statement against interest with an opposing-party statement; this exception requires unavailability.

rules.title: Statement of Personal or Family History
rules.prompt_question: What personal or family history statements are admissible if the declarant is unavailable?
rules.rule_statement: A statement about the declarant's own birth, adoption, legitimacy, ancestry, marriage, divorce, relationship by blood, adoption, or marriage, or similar facts of personal or family history, and certain statements about another person concerning such matters if the declarant was related to or closely associated with the person, are admissible if the declarant is unavailable.
rules.application_example: A deceased grandmother's statement about a child's parentage may be admissible.
rules.common_trap: This is separate from reputation and family-record exceptions.

rules.title: Forfeiture by Wrongdoing
rules.prompt_question: When does forfeiture by wrongdoing apply?
rules.rule_statement: A statement offered against a party that wrongfully caused, or acquiesced in wrongfully causing, the declarant's unavailability as a witness, and did so intending that result, is admissible under the forfeiture-by-wrongdoing exception.
rules.application_example: A defendant intimidates a witness into fleeing before trial.
rules.common_trap: The party must have acted with intent to make the witness unavailable, not merely engaged in unrelated wrongful conduct.

rules.title: Residual Exception
rules.prompt_question: When may hearsay be admitted under the residual exception?
rules.rule_statement: A hearsay statement not specifically covered by Rule 803 or 804 may be admitted if it has equivalent circumstantial guarantees of trustworthiness, is offered as evidence of a material fact, is more probative on the point than any other evidence that can be obtained through reasonable efforts, and admitting it will best serve the purposes of the rules and the interests of justice, with reasonable pretrial notice to the adverse party.
rules.application_example: An unusual but highly reliable statement may be admitted when no specific exception fits.
rules.common_trap: This is a narrow safety valve, not a broad invitation to ignore the hearsay rule.

rules.title: Hearsay Within Hearsay
rules.prompt_question: When is hearsay within hearsay admissible?
rules.rule_statement: Hearsay within hearsay is admissible only if each layer of the combined statements conforms with a hearsay exception or exclusion.
rules.application_example: A business record containing a customer's statement needs both the business-record exception for the record and another exception for the customer's statement.
rules.common_trap: Do not stop after finding an exception for the outer layer.

rules.title: Any Party May Impeach
rules.prompt_question: Who may impeach a witness?
rules.rule_statement: Any party, including the party that called the witness, may attack the witness's credibility.
rules.application_example: A party may impeach its own witness with a prior inconsistent statement.
rules.common_trap: Substantive use of the impeaching statement requires a separate rule, such as a hearsay exclusion or exception.

rules.title: Prior Inconsistent Statement for Impeachment
rules.prompt_question: How may a witness be impeached with a prior inconsistent statement?
rules.rule_statement: A witness may be impeached by a prior inconsistent statement. If the witness is not the opposing party, extrinsic evidence of the statement is admissible only if the witness is given an opportunity to explain or deny it and the opposing party is given an opportunity to examine the witness about it, unless justice so requires.
rules.application_example: A witness who testifies that the light was green may be impeached with a prior statement that it was red.
rules.common_trap: If the prior statement is collateral, courts may bar extrinsic proof even though the witness may be asked about it.

rules.title: Bias, Interest, or Motive
rules.prompt_question: How may bias or interest be used to impeach a witness?
rules.rule_statement: A witness may be impeached by showing bias, interest, or motive to misrepresent, and extrinsic evidence is generally permitted if relevant.
rules.application_example: A witness may be impeached by showing he expects leniency from the prosecution.
rules.common_trap: Bias impeachment is never collateral in the same sense as many other attacks because it is always material to credibility.

rules.title: Sensory or Mental Defect
rules.prompt_question: How may sensory or mental defects impeach a witness?
rules.rule_statement: A witness may be impeached by showing defects in perception, memory, or narration, including impaired vision, hearing, intoxication, or mental condition affecting the ability to observe or recall.
rules.application_example: Cross-examination reveals the eyewitness had no glasses and poor lighting at the scene.
rules.common_trap: This is not character impeachment and is usually proved through direct or extrinsic evidence if relevant.

rules.title: Character for Truthfulness
rules.prompt_question: How may character for truthfulness be used to impeach?
rules.rule_statement: A witness's credibility may be attacked or supported by testimony about the witness's reputation for having a character for truthfulness or untruthfulness, or by opinion testimony about that character. But evidence of truthful character is admissible only after the witness's character for truthfulness has been attacked.
rules.application_example: A witness may be impeached by another witness who testifies that the first witness has a reputation for dishonesty.
rules.common_trap: Specific instances are generally not admissible through extrinsic evidence to prove character for truthfulness, except on cross under Rule 608(b).

rules.title: Specific Acts on Cross-Examination
rules.prompt_question: When may specific acts be used to attack truthfulness?
rules.rule_statement: Except for criminal convictions under Rule 609, extrinsic evidence is not admissible to prove specific instances of a witness's conduct in order to attack or support the witness's character for truthfulness. But on cross-examination the court may allow inquiry into specific instances if they are probative of the character for truthfulness or untruthfulness of the witness or another witness whose character the witness being cross-examined has testified about.
rules.application_example: A witness may be asked on cross about past fraudulent acts if probative of truthfulness.
rules.common_trap: If the witness denies the act, the examiner must generally take the answer and cannot prove it with extrinsic evidence.

rules.title: Impeachment by Criminal Conviction
rules.prompt_question: When may a witness be impeached with a criminal conviction?
rules.rule_statement: For any witness, evidence of a felony conviction must be admitted, subject to Rule 403 in a civil case or when the witness is not a criminal defendant, and subject to a stricter reverse-403 balancing when the witness is a criminal defendant. Any conviction for a crime involving a dishonest act or false statement must be admitted, regardless of punishment, subject to the ten-year limit and other narrow exceptions.
rules.application_example: A prior perjury conviction is automatically admissible for impeachment, subject to timing limits.
rules.common_trap: Convictions more than ten years old are admissible only if their probative value substantially outweighs prejudicial effect and advance notice is given.

rules.title: Juvenile Adjudications
rules.prompt_question: Are juvenile adjudications admissible for impeachment?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of a juvenile adjudication is generally not admissible to impeach, but in a criminal case the court may allow it against a witness other than the accused if an adult conviction for that offense would be admissible and admitting the evidence is necessary to fairly determine guilt or innocence.
rules.application_example: A key prosecution witness's juvenile fraud adjudication may sometimes be admitted if fairness requires it.
rules.common_trap: The rule is much narrower than for adult convictions.

rules.title: Conviction on Appeal
rules.prompt_question: May a conviction on appeal still be used for impeachment?
rules.rule_statement: A conviction that satisfies Rule 609 is admissible for impeachment even if an appeal is pending, and evidence of the pending appeal is also admissible.
rules.application_example: A witness can be impeached with a fraud conviction even while appealing it.
rules.common_trap: The appeal goes to weight, not admissibility.

rules.title: Religious Beliefs
rules.prompt_question: May religious beliefs be used to attack or support credibility?
rules.rule_statement: Evidence of a witness's religious beliefs or opinions is not admissible to attack or support the witness's credibility.
rules.application_example: Counsel cannot argue a witness is more believable because she is devoutly religious.
rules.common_trap: Religious affiliation may still be admissible for a different purpose, such as bias.

rules.title: Rehabilitation of Witnesses
rules.prompt_question: When may a witness be rehabilitated?
rules.rule_statement: A witness may be rehabilitated after an attack on credibility, including by prior consistent statements when allowed, or by evidence of truthful character only after the witness's character for truthfulness has been attacked.
rules.application_example: After impeachment by a charge of recent fabrication, a prior consistent statement predating the alleged motive may rehabilitate the witness.
rules.common_trap: A party cannot bolster a witness before the witness has been attacked.

rules.title: Juror Testimony to Impeach Verdict
rules.prompt_question: When may a juror testify to impeach a verdict?
rules.rule_statement: During an inquiry into the validity of a verdict or indictment, a juror generally may not testify about statements made or incidents that occurred during deliberations, the effect of anything on a juror's vote, or a juror's mental processes. But a juror may testify about whether extraneous prejudicial information was improperly brought to the jury's attention, whether an outside influence was improperly brought to bear on a juror, or whether there was a mistake in entering the verdict on the verdict form.
rules.application_example: A juror may testify that a bailiff improperly pressured the jury.
rules.common_trap: Internal deliberation problems, misunderstanding the law, and mental processes are generally off-limits.

rules.title: Confrontation Clause
rules.prompt_question: What does the Confrontation Clause require?
rules.rule_statement: In criminal cases, the Sixth Amendment bars admission of testimonial hearsay against the accused unless the declarant is unavailable and the defendant had a prior opportunity to cross-examine the declarant, unless the defendant forfeited confrontation by wrongdoing.
rules.application_example: A formal witness affidavit prepared for prosecution cannot be admitted against the defendant without confrontation.
rules.common_trap: The Confrontation Clause applies only to testimonial statements offered against a criminal defendant, not to nontestimonial hearsay or evidence offered by the defense.

rules.title: What Is Testimonial?
rules.prompt_question: What kinds of statements are testimonial for Confrontation Clause purposes?
rules.rule_statement: Statements are testimonial when made with the primary purpose of creating an out-of-court substitute for trial testimony, such as formal statements to police during investigation, affidavits, depositions, prior testimony, and forensic certificates prepared for prosecution. Statements made during an ongoing emergency are generally nontestimonial.
rules.application_example: A lab certificate identifying a substance as cocaine prepared for trial is testimonial.
rules.common_trap: 911 calls can be either testimonial or nontestimonial depending on whether the primary purpose is to resolve an ongoing emergency or establish past facts for prosecution.

rules.title: Bruton Rule
rules.prompt_question: What is the Bruton rule?
rules.rule_statement: In a joint criminal trial, admission of a nontestifying codefendant's confession that facially incriminates the defendant violates the defendant's Confrontation Clause rights, even with a limiting instruction, unless the confession is properly redacted or the codefendant testifies.
rules.application_example: A codefendant's confession naming the defendant cannot be introduced in their joint trial if the codefendant does not testify.
rules.common_trap: Redactions that obviously point to the defendant may still violate Bruton.

rules.title: Compulsory Process and Defense Evidence
rules.prompt_question: What constitutional limit exists on excluding defense evidence?
rules.rule_statement: A criminal defendant has a constitutional right to present a defense, so evidentiary rules may not be applied arbitrarily or disproportionately to exclude critical, reliable defense evidence.
rules.application_example: A court may not mechanically exclude an otherwise reliable confession by a third party that exonerates the defendant if due process requires admission.
rules.common_trap: The right is not absolute; ordinary valid evidentiary rules still generally apply.
